skill.name: casely
skill.description: QA assistant for turning requirement documents and example test cases into parsed Markdown, style guides, planned test coverage, generated atomic test cases, and TestRail-ready Excel exports.
skill.summary: Generate QA test cases from requirements and export them to Excel.
skill.author: JohnWayneeee

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command and weak-crypto patterns, but review found the weak-crypto hits are false positives from Markdown, strings, and variable names. The real risks are legitimate local script execution, dependency installation guidance, document parsing, and filesystem reads and writes inside user projects. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential access, data exfiltration, or malicious network behavior.

medium_findings.title: Local Script Execution and Dependency Installation
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to install or verify Python dependencies with uv and run local parser and exporter scripts. This is legitimate for a QA document workflow, but it allows package installation and script execution in the user workspace, so users should review dependencies and paths before running commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions clearly call for dependency management and script execution, and the scripts exist. The behavior matches the skill purpose, so the concern is operational risk rather than malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Workspace File Read and Write Operations
medium_findings.description: The parser reads user-supplied documents and writes parsed Markdown files, while the exporter reads Markdown files and writes Excel workbooks. These filesystem operations are expected, but incorrect paths or untrusted documents could overwrite generated outputs or expose project contents to the active model context.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file reads, directory creation, and workbook writes are direct in the script code. The operations are scoped by user-provided or project paths and appear functional, not malicious.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Repository URL in Metadata
low_findings.description: The only hardcoded URL found is a GitHub repository URL in skill metadata. It does not initiate a network request or transmit data, so this is informational rather than a security issue.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is static metadata and no reviewed code uses it for outbound requests. This strongly supports a false-positive network classification.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm findings do not correspond to hashing or cryptographic code in the reviewed locations. They appear to be triggered by Markdown-related text, file extensions, or ordinary variable names such as md_file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain descriptions, headings, filenames, or Markdown references rather than cryptographic primitives. No evidence of MD5, SHA1, or password hashing was found in these contexts.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Detections Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Most external command detections are inline code spans, slash-command descriptions, or fenced CLI examples in documentation. They are not Ruby backtick execution and do not execute by themselves, although related workflow commands are covered in the medium findings.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are Markdown documentation and command examples, not executable Ruby code. The static pattern label does not match the file type or syntax.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Files Processed by Third-Party Document Parser
dangerous_patterns.description: The parser sends files from project input directories into docling for conversion. This is expected behavior, but complex office, PDF, and image parsing can increase exposure to parser bugs or resource exhaustion from untrusted files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The parser explicitly accepts many file formats and converts them with docling. This is a known class of operational risk, but no exploit or malicious handling is present.

content.user_title: Generate QA Test Cases from Requirements
content.value_statement: QA teams spend significant time converting requirements into consistent test cases. Casely parses project documents, learns the existing test style, generates atomic cases, and exports Excel files for test management systems.
actual_capabilities: Creates a project folder structure for requirements, examples, processed files, results, and exports.
Converts PDF, DOCX, XLSX, image, text, HTML, and Markdown inputs into parsed Markdown.
Builds a test style guide from existing example test cases and their table columns.
Plans coverage by module, level, estimated case count, test type, and tooling needs.
Generates atomic Markdown test cases that follow the detected style guide.
Exports generated Markdown test cases into formatted XLSX workbooks.
limitations: Requires Python dependencies such as docling and openpyxl to be installed before scripts can run.
Output quality depends on clear requirements and representative example test cases.
The skill does not validate business correctness without human QA review.
Excel export expects generated Markdown tables with a consistent horizontal structure.

use_cases.title: Create Test Suites from Requirements
use_cases.description: Convert feature specifications into structured functional, negative, integration, or boundary test cases.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Test Case Format
use_cases.description: Extract column order, tone, and formatting rules from existing examples before generating new cases.

use_cases.title: Prepare Excel Imports for TMS Tools
use_cases.description: Export one generated Markdown test case per XLSX workbook for review or import workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Start a QA Project
prompt_templates.prompt: Initialize a new Casely project named Checkout. Create the expected folders and tell me where to place requirements and example test cases.

prompt_templates.title: Parse Project Documents
prompt_templates.prompt: Parse the documents in my current Casely project. Report which files were converted and which files need attention.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Style Guide
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the parsed example test cases and create a style guide that preserves every column, order, and writing pattern.

prompt_templates.title: Plan, Generate, and Export
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the parsed requirements and style guide, plan critical coverage, generate functional and negative atomic cases, then export the results to XLSX.

output_examples.input: A QA engineer asks Casely to analyze parsed payment requirements and generate functional cases.
output_examples.output: Casely identifies payment modules, estimates coverage, creates atomic test case files, and reports the next recommended generation step.

output_examples.input: A QA lead provides existing test case examples and requests a team style guide.
output_examples.output: Casely summarizes the detected columns, required fields, naming patterns, language style, and formatting rules for future cases.

output_examples.input: A test manager asks to export completed cases for a test management system.
output_examples.output: Casely converts generated Markdown tables into formatted Excel workbooks and places them in the project export folder.

best_practices: Provide representative example test cases before generating new suites.
Review the generated style guide before planning or generating test cases.
Run exports only after checking that generated Markdown tables match the desired columns.
anti_patterns: Do not generate final cases from vague requirements without clarifying missing behavior.
Do not change column names during generation unless the style guide was updated first.
Do not run parser or export commands against unrelated workspace directories.

faq.question: What documents can Casely process?
faq.answer: It is designed for PDF, DOCX, PPTX, XLSX, HTML, text, Markdown, and common image formats supported by the parser.

faq.question: Does Casely create test cases automatically?
faq.answer: Yes. It can plan and generate atomic cases after requirements and a style guide are available.

faq.question: Can it match my team format?
faq.answer: Yes. It analyzes example test cases and keeps the same columns, order, tone, and table structure.

faq.question: Does it export to TestRail directly?
faq.answer: No. It exports formatted XLSX files that can be reviewed or adapted for test management imports.

faq.question: Does it need local scripts?
faq.answer: Yes. Parsing and Excel export use local Python scripts with docling and openpyxl dependencies.

faq.question: Should generated tests be reviewed?
faq.answer: Yes. QA review is needed to confirm coverage, business rules, and expected results.
